title: The Fallout

jokes.text: 'Mistrial! Mistrial!' / 'Yes, yes, I missed your trial!'
jokes.setup: The courtroom erupts with cries of 'Mistrial! Mistrial!' — a legal term for a trial invalidated by error.
jokes.punchline: Someone mishears or willfully misreads it as 'I missed your trial,' turning a legal crisis into a personal admission of absence.
jokes.explanation: A classically tight AD pun — two meanings colliding in a single sound, perfectly economical, and it lands as a genuine surprise. The chaos of the courtroom makes the misunderstanding plausible and funnier.

jokes.text: 'Had I known that she was talking about Lindsay, I would have been, like, "Goodbye, Felicia."'
jokes.setup: Michael probes Lucille about her reaction to Mimi, suggesting she thought Mimi was claiming Lucille's own child.
jokes.punchline: Lucille dismisses the idea of losing Lindsay with the slang 'Goodbye, Felicia' — a culturally jarring phrase from Lucille Bluth that reveals she wouldn't have cared at all about losing her daughter.
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: the absurdity of Lucille using millennial slang and the devastating character revelation that she would have happily let Lindsay go. High character integration — this could only be Lucille.

jokes.text: 'Is it true? Did you push Mimi down the stairs?' / 'I did, but nothing's gonna happen to me 'cause I'm just a boy.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks Buster point-blank if he pushed Mimi down the stairs, expecting deflection or denial.
jokes.punchline: Buster casually confirms it and immediately offers his non-defense: 'I'm just a boy' — a remembered hypnotic suggestion delivered as sincere logic.
jokes.explanation: This is premium AD joke construction. Buster's non sequitur 'I'm just a boy' reads as both a hypnotically-implanted memory and a genuine expression of his eternal arrested development (the show's thesis). The deadpan delivery of a murder confession as if it's a parking violation is perfectly calibrated. Justifies 8+ — this is what Buster's character is designed for.

jokes.text: Buster repeats 'I did it, but nothing's gonna happen to me because I'm just a boy' verbatim — and Michael responds 'I heard that already, pal.'
jokes.setup: Buster has just delivered the 'I'm just a boy' line once.
jokes.punchline: He repeats it identically, and Michael's exasperated 'I heard that already, pal' acknowledges the absurdity without breaking the scene.
jokes.explanation: The repetition escalates the joke rather than deflating it — Buster's inability to produce any other thought is funnier the second time. Michael's impatient acknowledgment is the perfect straight-man beat.

jokes.text: 'She's so mean. I wish I could shove her down the st...' 'Hey! Shh! No more talking about shoving, pushing...' 'No touching!' 'Or touching.'
jokes.setup: Buster starts complaining about Lottie, the prosecutor, and his instinct is immediately to wish violence upon her.
jokes.punchline: Michael frantically stops him mid-sentence ('shove her down the st—'), then Dusty adds 'No touching!' as a callback to Arrested Development's own running gag, and Michael incorporates it into his legal advice.
jokes.explanation: Multi-layered: Buster's compulsion to push women down stairs is darkly funny, the mid-sentence cutoff is well-timed, and integrating the franchise's own 'No touching!' gag into a legal strategy discussion is meta and earned. Near-8 quality.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, feel it. It's a rental.'
jokes.setup: The group is discussing serious legal matters; Michael says 'No touching' and Dusty echoes it.
jokes.punchline: Dusty breaks the tension by noting the furniture is a rental — implying he was more concerned about the chairs than the murder defense.
jokes.explanation: A quick, efficient joke about Dusty's misplaced priorities. Works as a character beat more than a crafted gag — competent but not exceptional.

jokes.text: 'What you saw in court today is gonna put you away.' — Michael corrects Buster's optimism about the trial.
jokes.setup: Buster says 'What I saw today in court blew me away.'
jokes.punchline: Michael pivots: 'What you saw in court today is gonna put you away.' The rhyme and brutal reframe land together.
jokes.explanation: Clean setup/punchline with internal rhyme ('blew me away'/'put you away'). The economy is excellent — maximum information with maximum irony in one line.

jokes.text: Gob's 'body-shaped thing' from a magician described as 'part of his final farewell disappearing act' — the narrator euphemistically explains Gob handed Buster what is strongly implied to be Tony Wonder's corpse.
jokes.setup: Buster was caught on tape dumping a 'person-shaped thing' into the sea. The narrator explains where it came from.
jokes.punchline: It came from Gob, who described it as 'part of a magician's final farewell disappearing act' — which is technically true in the darkest possible way, since Tony Wonder may have been murdered.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's euphemism is doing enormous work — 'final farewell disappearing act' is technically accurate and deeply ominous. The dark comedy here is earned because of seasons of Tony Wonder setup.

jokes.text: The narrator explains Gob was 'left with the necessity of pretending he was a gay man' after the sex-pref-swap illusion — 'He's gonna have to stay gay.'
jokes.setup: Gob participated in a 'sex-pref-swap illusion' with Tony Wonder, hoping it would cement their relationship.
jokes.punchline: The illusion went wrong in an 'unexpected way' — leaving Gob in the position of having to publicly perform being gay indefinitely.
jokes.explanation: AD at its layered best — the absurdity of 'sex-pref-swap illusion' as a phrase, plus the completely logical-within-the-show consequence of Gob having to maintain a false identity. Rewards rewatchers.

jokes.text: 'Just for seven years. Then we'll let you age out.' — The Gay Mafia offers Gob a timeline for his mandatory gayness.
jokes.setup: The Gay Mafia has confronted Gob for pretending to be gay. They're threatening him.
jokes.punchline: Rather than violence, they offer a bureaucratic solution — he can 'age out' in seven years, as if homosexuality is a gang membership with contractual terms.
jokes.explanation: The absurdist logic is airtight — applying literal gang rules to sexual orientation creates a completely ridiculous but internally consistent world. 'Age out' is the perfect word choice.

jokes.text: 'It's a lot more generous than what I did to that last guy who faked being gay, your pal Tony Wonder.'
jokes.setup: The Gay Mafia is explaining their leniency with Gob.
jokes.punchline: The implicit threat is that Tony Wonder was killed (or worse) for the same offense Gob committed — casually dropped as a matter of fact.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully casual delivery of an extremely dark implication. The 'your pal' framing is a nice menacing touch. Strong rewatch value as Tony Wonder is later revealed alive.

jokes.text: 'But Dusty thinks we're better off just ignoring it.' / 'Yeah, I think that's a bad idea. And I'm a little concerned about Dusty.'
jokes.setup: Buster is referencing Dusty's legal advice about the body-dumping footage.
jokes.punchline: Michael, who is not a lawyer, critiques Dusty's legal strategy — but Dusty, also not a lawyer, has been entrusted with a murder defense.
jokes.explanation: Functional joke about the absurdity of Dusty as defense attorney, but it's doing setup work more than delivering a standalone laugh.

jokes.text: 'Mm-hmm. Yeah, I saw A Few Good Men, too.'
jokes.setup: Michael appears to be doing a dramatic courtroom speech moment, perhaps channeling Tom Cruise's famous 'I want the truth!' scene.
jokes.punchline: Buster recognizes the reference and deflates it with the casual acknowledgment that he's also seen the movie.
jokes.explanation: Sets up the 'Few Good Men' running gag that pays off massively later. The joke here is mild but the plant is important. Buster's casual awareness is funny given his general obliviousness.

jokes.text: Michael manipulates Buster into revealing the cottage location by mentioning that Dusty gets to call Lucille 'Mother.' Buster immediately caves: 'Oh, they went to the cottage. I'd check the cottage.'
jokes.setup: Michael says he won't press Buster for information, then offhandedly mentions Dusty can call Lucille 'Mother.'
jokes.punchline: Buster's jealousy over the 'Mother' privilege is so acute that he immediately betrays the secret location, despite supposedly protecting it.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character comedy — Buster's pathological attachment to being Lucille's primary child is so perfectly calibrated that it functions as both the joke and the plot mechanism. Michael weaponizing Buster's Oedipal complex is the funniest possible way to extract information. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'She asked you to get rid of family memories?' / 'It's just that, typically, the drinking does that.'
jokes.setup: Dusty reveals Lucille told him to get rid of 'family tapes' and is confused why that would be unusual.
jokes.punchline: Michael explains that Lucille doesn't usually need to manually destroy memories — the drinking takes care of it.
jokes.explanation: Sharp Lucille character joke — her alcoholism as a practical memory-destruction tool is a dark observation delivered with perfect economy. Dusty's confusion amplifies it.

jokes.text: 'Oh, that's good, because she called it "evidence." But I don't think you're supposed to admit to destroying evidence in court.'
jokes.setup: Dusty has just been explaining his task, and Michael made a joke about Lucille's drinking.
jokes.punchline: Dusty casually reveals Lucille literally called the tapes 'evidence' and then notes — with apparently genuine uncertainty — that destroying evidence might be legally problematic.
jokes.explanation: Dusty's incompetence as a lawyer is the joke, but the structure is elegant — Lucille's casual criminality (calling evidence 'evidence') + Dusty's belated legal instinct = compounding absurdity.

jokes.text: 'George Bluth, Affair: Caught in Act' / 'George Bluth, Another Affair: Caught in Act, Hi-Res'
jokes.setup: They've found a box of family videotapes and are reading the labels.
jokes.punchline: George's affairs are so numerous and well-documented that they're filed with title, sequel designation ('Another Affair'), and even a production upgrade note ('Hi-Res').
jokes.explanation: Superb escalation joke — the labeling convention is funny, 'Another Affair' implies a series, and 'Hi-Res' is the killer detail, suggesting George was so systematic about being caught that he upgraded his equipment. Three-beat joke disguised as a list.

jokes.text: 'I know which one I would watch with a gun to my head.' / '...Not the hi-res.' / 'Oh.'
jokes.setup: Michael sets up a 'gun to my head' hypothetical about which tape he'd watch.
jokes.punchline: The expected answer would be 'the hi-res' (better quality). Instead, he'd still choose the standard definition — implying seeing his father's affair in HD is categorically worse.
jokes.explanation: Perfect two-beat punchline. The misdirection is clean (we expect hi-res = better) and the reveal (hi-res of dad's affair = worse) is immediately understood. Dusty's confused 'Oh.' is the ideal button. Justifies 8.

jokes.text: Michael and Dusty try to recall what happened in 1982 — Michael trails off, then: 'Awesome. '82 was awesome. Good year.'
jokes.setup: They find a tape labeled 'Buster, Age Eight, Under Hypnosis: 1982.' Dusty notes it's the year they tried to wean Buster, then Michael corrects him ('that was '83') and starts to recall '82, trailing into silence.
jokes.punchline: After a long, clearly uncomfortable pause, Michael overcorrects with 'Awesome. '82 was awesome. Good year' — obviously hiding something shameful about 1982.
jokes.explanation: The awkward silence is the joke — Michael's guilty over-enthusiasm ('Awesome. Good year.') after clearly blanking on something shameful is a well-observed character beat. Works on rewatch once you know what 1982 involves.

jokes.text: 'These are three-quarter-inch, Michael. This is not a TV station in the '70s.' / 'But, fortunately, Michael knew someone at a news station in his 70s.'
jokes.setup: Dusty explains they can't play the tapes because they're three-quarter-inch format.
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately pivots to '...in his 70s' — playing on the ambiguity of '70s' as decade vs. age to introduce John Beard.
jokes.explanation: Classic AD narrator wordplay at its finest — the switch from decade to age is instantaneous, elegant, and completely earned. The setup feeds directly into the punchline via the narrator, and it introduces plot information simultaneously. Near-perfect economy. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'I don't want to be too sentimental here, but these are my precious family evidence, so I should be the one that gets to destroy them.'
jokes.setup: Michael is volunteering to handle the tapes — ostensibly to save his brother.
jokes.punchline: He frames the tapes as 'precious family evidence' (contradictory terms) and insists he should be the one to destroy them — inverting the idea of sentimental value.
jokes.explanation: 'Precious family evidence' is a clever oxymoron and Michael's self-justifying logic is very on-brand. Competent character joke.

jokes.text: 'Could you really enjoy your freedom, knowing that you hurt my career?'
jokes.setup: Gob is trying to convince Buster not to reveal that the dumped 'body' was Tony Wonder's magic dummy.
jokes.punchline: Gob's moral argument isn't about Buster's freedom or justice — it's about Gob's magic career. The self-centeredness is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Pure Gob — his inability to think beyond his own interests even when a family member faces murder conviction is a consistent and funny character trait, perfectly expressed in one line.

jokes.text: 'And violate magician-assistant privilege? Not an option!'
jokes.setup: Buster suggests they could claim it was a random dummy without identifying it as Tony Wonder's.
jokes.punchline: Gob invokes 'magician-assistant privilege' — a completely invented legal concept presented with total conviction.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best single-line jokes. 'Magician-assistant privilege' is an absurdist invention that sounds just plausible enough (attorney-client privilege, doctor-patient privilege) to land perfectly. Gob's absolute conviction elevates it. Highly quotable, justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'Every show, every city... pending availability and parole conditions, of course.'
jokes.setup: Gob is offering Buster the dream of being his full-time assistant.
jokes.punchline: The grandiose offer is immediately undercut by the parenthetical 'pending availability and parole conditions' — both characters are potentially facing serious criminal consequences.
jokes.explanation: The legal caveat crashing into Gob's grandiose promise is a reliable AD structure — reality puncturing fantasy. Well-executed.

jokes.text: 'I don't know. I'll use Adhir.' — Gob's response to what happens if Buster is convicted.
jokes.setup: Buster asks 'But what if I'm convicted, Gob?'
jokes.punchline: Gob has already mentally moved on to his backup assistant — there's no consolation, no reassurance, just a logistical contingency plan.
jokes.explanation: Three words that tell you everything about Gob Bluth. The economy is extraordinary — 'I'll use Adhir' conveys complete indifference to Buster's potential imprisonment while also being funny precisely because 'Adhir' is a nobody we've never heard of. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: The Gay Mafia threatens: 'if Argyle isn't happy, the wall isn't gonna be the only thing covered in cement' — then the narrator reveals they were 'two tough guys right out of Guys and Dolls, a play they were in the middle of a very successful four-week run of at a local community theater.'
jokes.setup: Two intimidating Gay Mafia men deliver a sinister cement-related threat.
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately deflates their menace by revealing their theatrical day jobs — they're in a community theater production of Guys and Dolls.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The escalating cement threat ('covered in cement') is genuinely menacing — then the narrator undercuts it with 'Guys and Dolls at a local community theater.' The precision of 'a very successful four-week run' adds mock-journalistic detail that makes it funnier. The irony that Guys and Dolls IS literally a gangster musical is a sublime bonus layer. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: 'Reminds me of how Hanks walked around like Jim Lovell for a year.'
jokes.setup: One of the Guys and Dolls community theater gangsters makes an aside.
jokes.punchline: He references Tom Hanks staying in character as Jim Lovell (Apollo 13) for a year — an actor's commitment parallel that reveals these threatening men are deeply invested in their theatrical craft.
jokes.explanation: The callback to Guys and Dolls and the theatre nerd detail of 'how Hanks walked around like Jim Lovell' is a lovely character addition. These men are thugs who take their community theater VERY seriously.

jokes.text: Gob explains the wall illusion: 'I'm gonna go into the wall a gay man, which I am. If anyone from the Gay Mafia asks.'
jokes.setup: Gob describes his grand illusion — walking through the wall to be 'magically straightened out.'
jokes.punchline: He parenthetically qualifies 'which I am' (gay) with 'if anyone from the Gay Mafia asks' — acknowledging his deception while maintaining the fiction.
jokes.explanation: The conditional 'which I am. If anyone from the Gay Mafia asks.' is a beautifully economical confession of the whole plot situation, delivered as a throwaway qualifier.

jokes.text: 'Well, cis male, high butch.' / 'Gob had learned a lot in the short time he'd been magically gay.'
jokes.setup: Gob says he'll emerge from the wall 'magically straightened out' as his target identity.
jokes.punchline: He specifies 'cis male, high butch' — using identity terminology with great specificity, which the narrator frames as Gob's education during his 'magical' gayness.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's gentle mockery of Gob's sudden identity vocabulary is well-calibrated. 'High butch' as a goal is absurd but Gob-appropriate.

jokes.text: Gob makes Buster 'The Godfather' offer: 'Buster, you do this for me... one day... I'll come to you for a favor.' — with the extended dramatic pause.
jokes.setup: Gob sets up a dramatic, solemn Godfather-style favor offer.
jokes.punchline: The favor is: Gob will ask Buster for something someday. This is presented as a profound gift, but it's entirely backwards — it's a promise to make Buster do something for Gob again.
jokes.explanation: This is genuinely exceptional. The Godfather homage with the elongated pause builds real anticipation, and then the 'favor' is revealed to be: Gob will someday demand something of Buster. The complete inversion of generosity — framed as Gob's gift — is a perfect expression of Gob's narcissism. Quotable, structural, and character-specific. Among the episode's best.

jokes.text: 'Well, John, you used our house for To Entrap a Local Predator.' / 'That's it.' / 'Yeah.' / 'You were one of our super creeps.' / 'Yeah, yeah. Uh, no.'
jokes.setup: Michael and John Beard are getting reacquainted.
jokes.punchline: John remembers Michael as 'one of our super creeps' from the predator-trap show — implying he was caught as a pedophile suspect. Michael's 'Yeah, yeah. Uh, no.' is a slow-building denial.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the predator-trap show is a reliable AD laugh, and 'super creeps' as a show category is a darkly funny detail. Michael's three-beat denial ('Yeah, yeah. Uh, no.') is perfectly timed.

jokes.text: 'That's me! What am I doing in a Banana Stand shirt?'
jokes.setup: Michael sees himself on John Beard's footage from a night he doesn't remember.
jokes.punchline: Rather than reacting to the horror of seeing himself at a predator-trap location, Michael fixates on the Banana Stand shirt — a Bluth family detail.
jokes.explanation: Michael's misplaced priorities (shirt over location) is a good character beat, and the Banana Stand shirt is a meaningful detail that later connects to Cinco. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Dusty's courtroom examination of Gob: 'You and I are friends, don't you think?' / 'Objection. Is this going somewhere?' / 'Yeah, that's what I wanted to find out.'
jokes.setup: Dusty is cross-examining Gob as a witness — this is meant to be a legally strategic moment.
jokes.punchline: Dusty's opening question is about their personal friendship — not about the case. When asked if it's going somewhere, he admits he wanted to know.
jokes.explanation: Dusty asking his witness if they're friends as a court examination opening is perfect cringe comedy. The meta-logic ('that's what I wanted to find out') turns incompetence into accidental Zen. Near-8 caliber.

jokes.text: 'Hang out, like go to the beach or something?' / 'How does that pertain?' / 'I just want to know if he'll let me see his fort, because...' / 'I'll allow it.' / 'Oh.' / 'I won't allow that.'
jokes.setup: Dusty is pursuing his cross-examination of whether he and Gob will 'hang out.'
jokes.punchline: Escalating surrealism: beach → fort. The Judge allows 'hang out at the beach' but draws the line at the fort. The legal system has broken down completely.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'friends' to 'beach' to 'see his fort' perfectly illustrates Dusty's childlike mind. The Judge's selective allowing adds a surreal procedural layer.

jokes.text: Illusionist. Oh. I'm sorry. Oh, please. Sustained. I'll allow it.
jokes.setup: Lottie asks Gob if he's a 'magician by trade'; Gob corrects her
jokes.punchline: The judge simultaneously sustains Gob's correction (i.e., 'illusionist' is the appropriate term) but then also 'allows it' — two opposite rulings on the same point — the judge is clearly no longer functioning properly
jokes.explanation: A beloved running gag executed cleanly. The judge's contradictory 'sustained / I'll allow it' is a nice extra layer — the court has been so destabilized by Dusty that the judge is just saying things now.

jokes.text: Gob's testimony about Buster's skills inadvertently describes body disposal: 'He would just act all dumb, like, "I don't know what's happening, either." [...] She just... She vanished.'
jokes.setup: Gob is supposed to be vouching for Buster as a magic assistant — describing skills that exonerate him.
jokes.punchline: Every 'skill' he describes is a perfect description of how one would dispose of a body and evade suspicion — and Gob is doing it on the stand, under oath, in front of a jury.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's structural centerpiece joke. Gob's self-serving testimony — describing magic trick skills — is perfectly mapped onto body disposal methodology. The layers: Gob thinks he's helping, Buster realizes he's being buried (literally and legally), the jury is hearing a confession, and the audience sees all three simultaneously. Exceptional craft. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: 'Even if he was cutting a woman in half... with that steel.' / 'And then he'd make the steel disappear as well. So cool.'
jokes.setup: Gob is still describing Buster's magic skills to the jury.
jokes.punchline: The saw-woman-in-half trick is transparently re-described as murdering a woman, and then Gob adds 'so cool' — genuine enthusiasm for the magic while inadvertently endorsing the crime.
jokes.explanation: 'So cool' after describing a woman being cut in half is a perfect button. The overlap between magic trick and murder description is reaching full payoff here.

jokes.text: '...dumb-acting but... secretly sneaky guy.'
jokes.setup: Gob has been building to a character summation of Buster as an assistant.
jokes.punchline: The conclusion — 'dumb-acting but secretly sneaky' — is the most damning possible summary for a murder suspect, delivered with zero awareness by Gob.
jokes.explanation: The culminating punchline of the testimony sequence. 'Dumb-acting but secretly sneaky' is both an accurate description of Buster and a characterization that perfectly suggests premeditated crime. The pause structure ('dumb-acting but... secretly sneaky guy') shows good comedic timing even in text.

jokes.text: 'So... Gob forget-me-nowed me because of Tony. And that would be news on any other day, but I've gotta save my brother.'
jokes.setup: Michael has just discovered that Gob drugged him with a forget-me-now pill.
jokes.punchline: Rather than reacting with outrage, Michael treats being secretly drugged by his brother as just another Bluth family Tuesday — something that 'would be news on any other day.'
jokes.explanation: Michael's resigned acceptance that being drugged by Gob is a normal-level Bluth incident is both sad and funny. The 'any other day' qualifier does a lot of work.

jokes.text: Memory suppression? No. Not that, no. Give him some juice, please. Juice, please.
jokes.setup: The 1982 hypnosis tape plays; Tobias identifies his research as being about 'memory suppression'
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s panicked 'No. Not that, no' followed by the bizarre command 'Give him some juice' — a hypnosis-era tactic that functions like a nervous tic — is immediately funny and ominous
jokes.explanation: 'Juice, please' as a hypnosis interrupt code is immediately strange and funny. George Sr.'s frantic energy and the absurdity of juice as a control mechanism sets up the extended bit that follows.

jokes.text: Lucille on the tape: 'Even though we all know that I pushed Mimi down the stairs, if you tell anyone that, I'll go to jail. I don't want you to.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is mid-brainwashing session, trying to get Buster to confess to her crime.
jokes.punchline: She openly states the truth in front of the camera ('we all know that I pushed Mimi') and then immediately asks the child to lie about it — while being recorded — as if the confession doesn't count because it's said to an eight-year-old.
jokes.explanation: Lucille casually confessing murder on camera while gaslighting a child is both the darkest and funniest Lucille moment in the episode. Her total confidence that this recording won't matter is perfect character comedy.

jokes.text: 'What a bitch.' — Michael's reaction, watching the tape.
jokes.setup: Michael is watching his mother manipulate his child brother into confessing to her murder.
jokes.punchline: His entire response is 'What a bitch' — the most economical possible summary of Lucille's behavior, delivered with resigned finality.
jokes.explanation: Three words that earn their laugh through perfect placement. The economy is maximal — after 40 seconds of awful Lucille behavior, Michael's flat 'What a bitch' is exactly the right response, and its simplicity is the joke.

jokes.text: 'I wish I hadn't finished that juice.' — Young Buster, on the hypnosis tape.
jokes.setup: Young Buster has been successfully brainwashed into confessing to Mimi's murder.
jokes.punchline: His final words on the tape are about the juice — the same juice Tobias was demanding. Buster, not Tobias, drank the juice, and apparently wishes he hadn't.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best single-line button. The juice becomes the throughline of the entire hypnosis scene, and young Buster's parting words being about the juice — in the middle of being psychologically destroyed — is genuinely surprising and earns a huge laugh. Perfect callback structure and ultimate economy. Justifies 8.5.

jokes.text: 'A Few Good Men. Great movie.' — Michael responds to Dusty's 'I think it's time to find out if Mother can handle the truth.'
jokes.setup: Dusty says 'I think it's time to find out if Mother can handle the truth' — a Few Good Men reference.
jokes.punchline: Michael completes the reference evaluation with 'Great movie.' — apparently more excited about the film than the legal strategy.
jokes.explanation: Building the Few Good Men running gag — this instance is a mild laugh, a plant for the catastrophic payoff later.

jokes.text: The narrator: 'As, after Gob had outsmarted a dumb lawyer, the question was, could he out-dumb a smart lawyer?'
jokes.setup: The narrator sets up the next courtroom sequence.
jokes.punchline: The question is whether Gob can 'out-dumb a smart lawyer' — framing stupidity as a strategic asset, and suggesting Gob's courtroom strategy is competitive idiocy.
jokes.explanation: Sharp narrator line — 'outsmarted a dumb lawyer' / 'out-dumb a smart lawyer' is clean parallel construction with a genuine insight about Gob's capabilities.

jokes.text: Lottie asks if the objects Buster could hide were 'as big as a human body?' / 'Oh, God, yeah, yeah. Just, I mean, the... often, the exact same dimension of a human body... Often a female body. Any female body, really.'
jokes.setup: Lottie is using Gob's own testimony to build a case against Buster — asking leading questions about body-sized objects.
jokes.punchline: Gob, unable to stop talking, escalates from 'human body' to 'average human male or female body' to 'often a female body' to 'any female body, really' — somehow making it worse with each word.
jokes.explanation: The descending specificity of 'any female body, really' after 'often a female body' is perfectly awful. Gob's inability to stop digging despite visibly realizing what's happening ('I see what you're doing') is cringe-escalation at its finest.

jokes.text: 'I see what you're doing.' [beat] 'Yes, it would.' / 'No more questions.'
jokes.setup: Lottie asks if Buster's skills would be helpful 'for, say, covering up a crime.'
jokes.punchline: Gob acknowledges he sees the trap, pauses — and then answers 'Yes, it would' anyway, before Lottie immediately dismisses him.
jokes.explanation: This might be the episode's single funniest beat. Gob explicitly says 'I see what you're doing' — fully aware of the trap — and then falls into it anyway by saying 'Yes, it would.' Lottie's immediate 'No more questions' is the perfect button. The acknowledgment-before-failure structure is what elevates this above ordinary cringe comedy. Justifies near-9 craft.

jokes.text: 'Interesting, huh? Hers were a lot easier than his.' — Narrator after Lottie's cross-examination.
jokes.setup: Gob has destroyed Buster's defense with his own testimony. Now Lottie effortlessly confirms the same facts.
jokes.punchline: The narrator summarizes the grotesque disparity: Gob worked hard to inadvertently condemn Buster; Lottie just asked him one question.
jokes.explanation: Clean narrator button on the whole testimony sequence. Economy is the main strength.

jokes.text: 'Permission to interrupt the case.' / 'Could you identify yourself?' / 'You missed it. I killed up there.'
jokes.setup: Michael barges into the courtroom to help with Buster's defense.
jokes.punchline: Asked to identify himself, Michael's response is 'You missed it. I killed up there' — using 'killed' in the stand-up comedy sense ('killed it') while simultaneously being at a murder trial.
jokes.explanation: The 'killed' wordplay in a murder trial context is solid AD wordplay. Not the sharpest in the episode but well-deployed.

jokes.text: 'Oh, that'll be great timing, because I've been drinking this free court water.' / 'Does the prosecution have any objections?' / 'I need a break.' / 'Oh, let him go. Oh, he worked through recess.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks to join the defense. The court goes through the formalities.
jokes.punchline: Dusty's contribution to the moment is noting he's been drinking free court water; Lottie's objection is that she needs a bathroom break; the Judge's sympathy ('he worked through recess') treats it like a kindergarten situation.
jokes.explanation: Three simultaneous character jokes in quick succession — Dusty's water preoccupation, Lottie's bodily function objection, and the Judge treating court like elementary school. Solid ensemble absurdism.

jokes.text: 'I would like to call Lucille Bluth, the accused mother.' / 'I think you mean "the accused's mother."' / 'Oh, what did I say?'
jokes.setup: Michael announces his witness.
jokes.punchline: He accidentally (or perhaps correctly) calls Lucille 'the accused mother' — which the Judge corrects, but Michael's genuine 'Oh, what did I say?' suggests he may not fully realize he was right the first time.
jokes.explanation: Superb double-meaning joke — 'the accused mother' works both as a grammatical error and as an accurate description of Lucille (an accused person who is also a terrible mother). Michael's confusion makes the ambiguity land harder. Justifies 8.

jokes.text: Lucille's response to being questioned: 'Look at you, milking this for every moment. Isn't this classic Michael? We make fun of him all the time. Right, Gob?'
jokes.setup: Michael asks Lucille to state her relationship to the defendant.
jokes.punchline: Rather than answering, Lucille launches into a familiar family critique of Michael — trying to undercut him in front of the courtroom as if it were a dinner table.
jokes.explanation: Lucille treating the courtroom as a family gathering and attempting to recruit Gob against Michael is perfectly in character. The snap to Gob for corroboration is a nice touch.

jokes.text: 'The rift between the brothers had been repaired.' — Narrator, as Gob backs up Lucille.
jokes.setup: Lucille appeals to Gob to back her up against Michael.
jokes.punchline: The narrator notes that the brothers' rift has been 'repaired' — but the repair is them uniting against Michael, not with him.
jokes.explanation: Clean ironic narrator beat — the 'rift repaired' is technically accurate but practically the opposite of helpful. Economy is the main strength.

jokes.text: 'Well, he did confess to it.' — Lucille's response to Michael's assertion that Buster may not have pushed Mimi.
jokes.setup: Michael is building toward revealing Lucille's guilt — suggesting Buster may be innocent of Mimi's death.
jokes.punchline: Lucille points out that Buster did confess — knowing full well she engineered that confession under hypnosis.
jokes.explanation: Lucille citing the confession she personally engineered as evidence against her son is a brilliantly cynical callback. Her total composure is frightening and funny.

jokes.text: So it seems that I came home from somewhere. The point is that we're talking about you, not me.
jokes.setup: Michael questions where Lucille was the night Lucille 2 disappeared; Lucille is confronted with evidence
jokes.punchline: 'It seems that I came home from somewhere' is a magisterially evasive non-answer — Lucille acknowledges being somewhere without specifying where — followed by her instantly trying to redirect
jokes.explanation: 'It seems that I came home from somewhere' is quintessential Lucille — acknowledging physical reality while giving away nothing. The construction ('seems that I came') is perfectly evasive and perfectly her. Highly quotable character moment.

jokes.text: Michael's courtroom examination of Lucille spirals into him accidentally confessing to murder: 'I think that I'm the killer.' / 'And that's how Michael A Few Good Men-ed himself.'
jokes.setup: Michael has been setting up the Few Good Men dramatic truth-extraction moment throughout the episode, quoting the film repeatedly.
jokes.punchline: Instead of breaking Lucille, Michael talks himself into confessing to the murder under cross-examination of himself — and the narrator names the trope he fell into.
jokes.explanation: The episode's crown jewel. Every Few Good Men reference has been a plant for this moment — Michael's movie-informed courtroom fantasy backfires so completely that he confesses to a murder he didn't commit. 'That's how Michael A Few Good Men-ed himself' as a narrator-named comedic category is exceptional. This is AD at its best: running gag → setup → catastrophic callback payoff. Comparable to the best AD moments. Justifies 9+.

jokes.text: 'You're goddamn right I did!' [beat] 'And that's how Michael A Few Good Men-ed himself. I think.'
jokes.setup: Michael has shouted a murder confession in court.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's 'I think' — a moment of uncertainty — suggests even the omniscient narrator isn't sure if Michael actually did it, adding a layer of genuine ambiguity to what was meant to be a comic beat.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's 'I think' is doing extraordinary work — it simultaneously deflates the drama with uncertainty and raises genuine questions about whether Michael is guilty. The Ron Howard narrator being unsure is meta and unsettling in the best way.

jokes.text: I think. I, uh... I... I, uh...
jokes.setup: Michael has just confessed to murder in open court
jokes.punchline: The extended, stuttering non-recovery — 'I think. I, uh... I... I, uh...' — is pure comedic deflation after a massive self-inflicted disaster
jokes.explanation: The physical comedy of Michael's paralysis after the confession is implied and lands well as a reaction beat. Not a joke in itself but earns its placement as the aftermath of a great joke.

jokes.text: Nice humblebrag.
jokes.setup: Michael, spiraling through his accidental confession, mentions that he 'offered to sleep with her so she would forget my $700,000 debt'
jokes.punchline: 'Nice humblebrag' — pointing out that framing prostituting oneself as an act of shame is actually a brag about one's desirability
jokes.explanation: The 'humblebrag' call-out is well-timed and cuts through Michael's spiral with a sharp observation. 'Nice humblebrag' as a two-word verdict on offering sexual favors for debt forgiveness is funny and precise.

jokes.text: 'The opposing counsel... is confessing.' / 'No further confessions, Your Honor.'
jokes.setup: Michael has confessed to murder in the middle of his own cross-examination of Lucille.
jokes.punchline: Lottie identifies the situation with total bewilderment ('The opposing counsel is confessing'), and the Judge's response is not to end the trial but to issue a procedural rule: 'No further confessions.'
jokes.explanation: 'No further confessions, Your Honor' is a brilliantly specific judicial ruling that implies this happens regularly enough to need a standing order. Lottie's diagnostic statement is the perfect straight-man beat.

jokes.text: No further confessions, Your Honor.
jokes.setup: After Michael continues confessing, Lottie rests her case
jokes.punchline: 'No further confessions, Your Honor' — phrased as 'no further questions' but for the unprecedented situation where the opposing lawyer keeps confessing
jokes.explanation: Exceptional button. The substitution of 'confessions' for 'questions' in the standard courtroom phrase is elegant and devastating. It lands because it follows the prior observation perfectly and turns a legal convention into an absurd comment on Michael's self-destruction. Very quotable.

jokes.text: The narrator describes Michael's confession as 'a real Making of a Monster-type moment.'
jokes.setup: Michael has just dramatically confessed to murder in open court.
jokes.punchline: The narrator frames it using a true-crime documentary genre reference — 'Making of a Monster-type moment' — treating Michael's catastrophic self-destruction as a TV production beat.
jokes.explanation: Meta reference that works in context — the narrator narrating the family like a documentary crew makes this kind of genre callout appropriate and funny.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'I couldn't have laid it out any more clearly for you. All you had to do was get me on the stand and make me look like a liar. How do you screw that up?'
jokes.setup: After the catastrophic courtroom sequence, Lucille confronts Michael privately.
jokes.punchline: Lucille reveals she engineered the entire situation — she wanted Michael to expose her as a liar (about Mimi) to prove Buster is innocent. Michael's heroic intervention was her plan, and he ruined it by confessing.
jokes.explanation: This is a masterclass in AD plot construction as comedy. Everything Michael thought he was doing (exposing Lucille, saving Buster) was exactly what Lucille planned — and Michael ruined it by accidentally confessing. Lucille's exasperation ('How do you screw that up?') reframes the entire courtroom sequence as his failure, not his heroism. Near-perfect character integration. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: 'But I didn't push Mimi down the stairs. Buster did.' / 'Mom, you're on tape admitting that you did it.' / [...] 'We were implanting a memory. We didn't want him growing up thinking he was a killer. Instead, he thinks he's a hero.'
jokes.setup: Michael challenges Lucille on the tape evidence.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's explanation reveals the entire psychology was backwards — they gave Buster a heroic false memory to protect him, but the actual killer (Buster) grew up thinking he was a hero, while Michael (innocent) grew up thinking he's heroic and turned out to be... something.
jokes.explanation: The irony of the Bluth psychology project's actual result — Buster as a hero, Michael as a mess — is darkly funny and structurally satisfying. 'Instead, he thinks he's a hero' delivered about a murderer is the key punch.

jokes.text: 'Meanwhile, I grow up thinking I'm a hero, and I turn out to be a killer.'
jokes.setup: Michael is processing the revelation about Buster's implanted memory.
jokes.punchline: Michael draws the parallel to himself — he grew up thinking he was the family hero, but has just confessed to murder in open court. The parallel is both funny and genuinely sad.
jokes.explanation: This line is doing double comedy and genuine pathos simultaneously — the joke is that Michael's heroic self-image has resulted in a murder confession; the sadness is that it might be true. The structural parallel (Buster = killer who thinks he's hero / Michael = hero who thinks he's killer) is elegant. Justifies 8.5.

jokes.text: 'Turns out, I'm the worst. I'm the worst Bluth.' / 'Never say that!' / 'Gob is the worst.' / 'Yeah. Yeah. Yeah.'
jokes.setup: Michael is in a moment of self-recrimination, declaring himself 'the worst Bluth.'
jokes.punchline: Lucille's reflex is to defend the family name — then immediately agrees Gob is the worst, with both of them nodding along in solemn agreement.
jokes.explanation: Perfect beat. The setup ('Never say that!') seems like a moment of maternal warmth — then Lucille redirects it to Gob with zero transition. The triple 'Yeah. Yeah. Yeah.' as shared agreement is the ideal button. Highly quotable, strong character integration.

jokes.text: 'I don't know who let these libtards in here.' / 'I beg your pardon?' / 'People with liberal-tardation.'
jokes.setup: Protesters have appeared at the wall demonstration, and Lucille objects to their presence.
jokes.punchline: Lucille uses 'libtards,' and when Sally objects, Lucille provides a portmanteau definition — 'people with liberal-tardation' — as if she's coined a neutral neologism.
jokes.explanation: Character-specific joke — only Lucille could say this with complete self-assurance. The 'definition' as a recovery move is the comedic beat. Somewhat dated slang diminishes originality score.

jokes.text: Tobias leading protesters: 'Go limp! Go limp! Go... No, no, no, no. You look like you're at a Walking Dead convention. No, limp, like, uh, flaccid. Unerect.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is leading protesters in a go-limp nonviolent resistance tactic.
jokes.punchline: The protesters are going limp in the wrong way — apparently zombie-style — and Tobias's corrections escalate from directorial to increasingly inappropriate vocabulary ('flaccid,' 'unerect').
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias — his instruction to 'go limp like flaccid, unerect' is simultaneously a protest direction and a very Tobias-specific double entendre. The Walking Dead comparison is a sharp specific visual. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'I can't believe my dry cleaners came.' — reacting to the protesters.
jokes.setup: Protesters have arrived, and the family is trying to identify who they are.
jokes.punchline: George's reaction reveals that his dry cleaners — apparently personal friends — are among the protesters, implying his social circle protests the Bluth family's projects.
jokes.explanation: Quick character joke that reveals George's social milieu and the absurdity of the situation. Economy is good.

jokes.text: Tobias is revealed to be a paid protester — and George Michael is paying him. 'Dad, I made you a chant follower, not a chant leader.'
jokes.setup: The protesters are being called out; it emerges that George Sr. is among them.
jokes.punchline: George Michael reveals he organized and paid the protesters — including his own father-figure — and is annoyed that George Sr. promoted himself to 'chant leader.'
jokes.explanation: 'Chant follower, not a chant leader' is a delightfully specific hierarchy in the world of paid protest. The revelation that George Michael is running the operation is a great misdirection payoff.

jokes.text: 'What is she paying you?' / 'Three dollars an hour and a copy of the news footage for my reel.' — Tobias reveals his protest payment.
jokes.setup: Sally confronts Tobias about being paid to protest.
jokes.punchline: Three dollars an hour is insultingly low; the 'copy for my reel' reveals Tobias is still pursuing his acting career and treats protest footage as audition material.
jokes.explanation: The acting reel detail is perfect Tobias — he's never not auditioning. The combination of exploitation (three dollars) and delusion (this as reel-worthy) is a reliable but well-executed character joke.

jokes.text: 'How'd you like to be a Bluth?' / 'Fully-blown?' / 'No.' — Lucille making an offer to Tobias.
jokes.setup: Lucille is trying to recruit Tobias to change protest sides.
jokes.punchline: Tobias's first question about becoming a 'fully-blown' Bluth is an accidental double entendre that Lucille firmly shuts down.
jokes.explanation: Standard Tobias double entendre — dependable laugh but not a new joke. Lucille's 'No.' is the right response.

jokes.text: 'And not your creepy girlfriend or your imbecilic son.' / 'Funny story, that. They left me.'
jokes.setup: Lucille adds conditions to the Bluth offer — Tobias can join, but not DeBrie or Murphybrown.
jokes.punchline: Tobias's 'Funny story, that. They left me.' — both the deflation of 'funny story' (it isn't) and the casual revelation of his family abandonment.
jokes.explanation: 'Funny story, that. They left me.' is perfectly calibrated — the 'funny story' introduction sets up something anecdotal, and the devastating 'they left me' is both the entire story and deeply sad. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: 'I wear the horns of a cuckold. That's why you don't put your girlfriend in the same sleeping bag with your son. But I'm not gonna let him share mine. I like to spread my legs.'
jokes.setup: Tobias explains the situation with DeBrie and Murphybrown leaving him.
jokes.punchline: Tobias uses 'horns of a cuckold' (archaically correct), explains the sleeping bag situation (they shared a sleeping bag and became romantically involved), and then 'I like to spread my legs' — an innocent reference to sleeping position that is of course a Tobias double entendre.
jokes.explanation: Three-part joke: 'horns of a cuckold' (Tobias's theatrical vocabulary), the sleeping bag wisdom (bizarre but internally logical), and 'spread my legs' (classic Tobias inadvertent double entendre). The triple beat makes this stronger than a single Tobias line.

jokes.text: 'Apparently, so does DeBrie.' — Lucille's response to 'I like to spread my legs.'
jokes.setup: Tobias says 'I like to spread my legs.'
jokes.punchline: Lucille's immediate 'Apparently, so does DeBrie' — implying sexual promiscuity with Murphybrown — is an unusually sharp Lucille comeback.
jokes.explanation: Lucille's deadpan delivery of a sexual comeback is perfectly timed. The economy is near-perfect — five words that land the joke completely.

jokes.text: Chinese investor: 'We are much more concerned with the software portion of the demonstration. We are very eager to see how it interfere with the broadcast. We need something like this in China. 'Cause your game shows are the worst.'
jokes.setup: George is pitching the wall to Chinese investors, emphasizing its security features.
jokes.punchline: The investors immediately pivot from national security/immigration (the obvious wall purpose) to game show censorship — the primary application they have in mind for the software.
jokes.explanation: 'Cause your game shows are the worst' as the stated reason for needing censorship software is a sharp non sequitur. The pivot from border security to game show criticism is unexpected and funny.

jokes.text: 'And, of course, this will be very helpful in avoiding the spread of democracy.'
jokes.setup: The investors are explaining their interest in the Fakeblock software.
jokes.punchline: The investor casually adds 'avoiding the spread of democracy' as a practical software application — stated as blandly as one might say 'blocking pop-up ads.'
jokes.explanation: The matter-of-fact delivery of autocracy as a software feature is well-calibrated dark comedy. The economy of expressing totalitarian ambition in seven words is the main strength.

jokes.text: 'But, fortunately, Gob invited me.' / 'Hey, Gob. I bet you think you're seeing me here.' / 'Yeah, you're part of the illusion.' / 'Okay, I'll meet you backstage. Yeah, I expect to see you there.' / 'Why are you...?' / 'I bet you didn't expect him to expect to see me back there.'
jokes.setup: Buster has arrived at the wall event and encounters Gob.
jokes.punchline: Their conversation is entirely about expectations of seeing each other — Gob thinks Buster is part of the illusion, Buster thinks Gob expects to see him — and neither is quite tracking the conversation.
jokes.explanation: The conversation where neither person is quite tracking the other is a good character beat — both Gob and Buster are operating from different realities. 'I bet you didn't expect him to expect to see me back there' is pleasantly confusing.

jokes.text: Yes, because I'm a good assistant, and unlike you, I know how to keep a secret. I can't believe you told Warden Gentles about our family secrets.
jokes.setup: Buster defends his decision to be part of Gob's illusion
jokes.punchline: Buster brags about keeping secrets immediately after the episode has shown he reveals everything whenever Lucille's maternal power is deployed against him
jokes.explanation: Buster's self-delusion about his secret-keeping abilities, immediately undercut by Lucille pointing out he told the warden family secrets, is a nice ironic beat. The 'I know how to keep a secret' claim is objectively false.

jokes.text: 'This is why you don't go to rehab. I lost all my tolerance.'
jokes.setup: Lucille explains why she told Warden Gentles about family secrets — she was drunk.
jokes.punchline: Her explanation is not remorse but a practical lesson: rehab ruins your alcohol tolerance, therefore rehab is inadvisable.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quotable lines. Lucille's anti-rehab logic is impeccable within her worldview: rehab makes you drunk faster, therefore don't go to rehab. The conclusion is monstrous and perfectly Lucille. Economy is near-perfect. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: Look, you wanted to get out for the parade, but I drank too much, okay? This is why you don't go to rehab. I lost all my tolerance.
jokes.setup: Lucille explains why she told Warden Gentles secrets
jokes.punchline: 'This is why you don't go to rehab. I lost all my tolerance.' — Lucille's moral of the story is that sobriety lowered her tolerance for alcohol, making her a worse drunk-secret-keeper
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. 'This is why you don't go to rehab. I lost all my tolerance.' is exceptional: it reframes sobriety as a tactical mistake in Lucille's criminal defense strategy. Only Lucille would draw this lesson from rehab. It's original, structurally tight, deeply character-specific (no other character could say this), economical, and earned through years of Lucille characterization. Comparable to the anchor examples. Strong candidate for the episode's top joke.

jokes.text: 'Mother, I know the truth now. I always thought you pushed Mimi down the stairs, and you made me take the blame. I got it backwards. I pushed Mimi down the stairs. And you made me take the blame. That's the same.'
jokes.setup: Buster has a moment of epiphany about the Mimi situation.
jokes.punchline: He works through the logic: 'I got it backwards. I pushed Mimi. You made me take blame. That's the same.' — Buster's 'correction' of his own understanding arrives at the identical conclusion with complete sincerity.
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best joke. Buster's triumphant moment of clarity — 'I got it backwards!' — followed by working out that it's exactly the same is structurally perfect. The comedy emerges from Buster's genuine effort to think, the false triumph of 'I got it backwards,' and the logical precision of 'That's the same.' It's a devastating character joke that also advances the plot. Perfectly integrates Buster's limited cognition with the show's themes. Comparable to top AD moments. Justifies 9.

jokes.text: 'Can you imagine how weird I would have turned out if I thought I did it?' / [beat] 'I really can't.'
jokes.setup: Buster expresses gratitude to Lucille for the false memory implantation, saying it saved him from knowing he was a killer.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's 'I really can't' — she genuinely cannot imagine Buster being any weirder, and the pause makes clear she's not being sarcastic about him being weird.
jokes.explanation: Exquisite. Buster's question assumes his baseline weirdness is normal; Lucille's 'I really can't' confirms it is not — and the acknowledgment that Buster is already too weird to meaningfully worsen is both devastating and tender. The rewatch bonus comes from knowing all of Buster's behavior across the series. Justifies 9.

jokes.text: 'Today, in these plywood forms, 10,000 pounds of wet, gay, QuickHard cement will be poured, because if we build it, they won't come!'
jokes.setup: Gob is giving the presentation speech for the wall.
jokes.punchline: 'Wet, gay, QuickHard cement' — each adjective is either a Gob sexuality reference or an absurdly specific product name. 'If we build it, they won't come' inverts Field of Dreams ('If you build it, they will come') for an immigration context.
jokes.explanation: Two jokes in one sentence: 'wet, gay, QuickHard' is Gob's sexuality leaking into product description, and 'they won't come' is a sharp Field of Dreams inversion. Good density.

jokes.text: 10,000 pounds of wet, gay, QuickHard cement will be poured, because if we build it, they won't come!
jokes.setup: Gob is presenting the wall to the crowd and investors
jokes.punchline: 'Wet, gay, QuickHard cement' — Gob has apparently been sourcing cement from the Gay Mafia's cement company, naming it in the presentation; 'QuickHard' as a product name is inadvertently sexual; 'if we build it, they won't come' inverts Field of Dreams
jokes.explanation: Multiple jokes packed in: 'gay' as product descriptor, 'QuickHard' as brand name (double entendre), and the Field of Dreams inversion ('they won't come'). The cement being 'gay' is a callback payoff. The 'QuickHard' name for Argyle's cement is a beautifully absurd detail. Density of comedy is high.

jokes.text: 'Oh, that's clever. All right, get ready to storm the wall.'
jokes.setup: Gob delivers 'If we build it, they won't come!' to the crowd.
jokes.punchline: Someone appreciates the wordplay ('Oh, that's clever') before immediately calling for a protest escalation ('storm the wall') — appreciating the wit while opposing the project.
jokes.explanation: A quick beat that separates aesthetic appreciation from political opposition — funny but not exceptional.

jokes.text: 'I don't know, I guess just stop tweeting from shithole countries' — Gob's description of the Fakeblock software's function.
jokes.setup: Gob is trying to describe what Fakeblock actually does.
jokes.punchline: His best description is 'stop tweeting from shithole countries' — a direct Trump-era reference (the 'shithole countries' comment was made in 2018) delivered as casual product description.
jokes.explanation: Topical reference that was sharp in 2018, somewhat dated now. The character integration is weak — this could be anyone's line. The vulgarity is cheap-ish.

jokes.text: Well, it is a bit of a flaw that anybody can get over our wall by using a rope ladder.
jokes.setup: Gob is climbing over the wall using a rope ladder to get to the other side for his illusion; protesters follow
jokes.punchline: Gob observes, in the middle of his own climb, that the wall's fundamental security flaw is rope ladder accessibility — a design critique of his own project during live demonstration
jokes.explanation: Gob's real-time, in-practice critique of the wall's security vulnerability — while using that very vulnerability — is a great self-defeating observation. The casualness of 'it is a bit of a flaw' is the joke.

jokes.text: 'How long until the clown's done with the clown show?' / 'He's... technically, he's not a clown. He's, um... Five minutes. Maybe six, if he has a hard time getting his shirt off.'
jokes.setup: The Chinese investors are impatient with Gob's magic show.
jokes.punchline: George Michael starts to defend Gob's professional title, abandons it mid-sentence, and then estimates the time based on 'if he has a hard time getting his shirt off' — revealing this is a known Gob variable.
jokes.explanation: 'If he has a hard time getting his shirt off' as a measurable time variable is perfect — it implies this has happened enough times to be a factor in scheduling. The abandoned title defense ('technically not a clown, he's um...') is also a great beat.

jokes.text: We have a word for this in Chinese. It means a combination of embarrassment and obligation.
jokes.setup: George Michael explains to the Chinese investors that he has to let Gob finish his embarrassing illusion because Gob is his uncle
jokes.punchline: The investor notes that Chinese has a specific word for the combination of embarrassment and obligation — implying this is a recognized enough experience to require dedicated vocabulary
jokes.explanation: A gentle, observational joke about cultural specificity — the idea that the Bluth family's behavior has generated a known phenomenon with dedicated vocabulary in another language. Gentle satire with a universal truth about family obligation.

jokes.text: 'We have a word for this in Chinese. It means a combination of embarrassment and obligation.'
jokes.setup: George Michael explains he has to let Gob finish the illusion because 'he's my uncle.'
jokes.punchline: The investor offers that Chinese has a word for the specific feeling George Michael is experiencing — family obligation that causes embarrassment — as if to validate this as a culturally recognized human experience.
jokes.explanation: The 'word for it in another language' joke structure is well-used here — the concept being described (embarrassing family obligation) is universal enough that the Chinese investor immediately has the word for it. Warm and observational.

jokes.text: 'I am now behind the wall, still fully gay. But in a moment, I'll be on the other side. So, ladies, prepare to be objectified. Ooh, mommy!'
jokes.setup: Gob is narrating his walk-through-wall transformation from gay to straight.
jokes.punchline: 'So, ladies, prepare to be objectified' as a promise of his heterosexual emergence — then immediately undermined by 'Ooh, mommy!' which is either the first sign of straightness or just Gob.
jokes.explanation: 'Ladies, prepare to be objectified' is a hilarious declaration of heterosexual intent — and 'Ooh, mommy!' is the perfect self-undermining button. Pure Gob.

jokes.text: 'I'm still gay... so I'm not very good with tools. So I have to use magic.'
jokes.setup: Gob is explaining how he'll get through the plywood form.
jokes.punchline: His explanation invokes the stereotype that gay men are bad with tools — and he uses it as a logical bridge to 'therefore: magic.'
jokes.explanation: The logic chain — 'still gay → bad with tools → therefore magic' — is Gob's internal reasoning at its most absurd and most in-character. The economy is excellent.

jokes.text: 'Oh, I'm craving a beer. My references are becoming more basic. I think Billy Eichner is mean.'
jokes.setup: Gob is inside the wall, going through his transformation from gay to straight.
jokes.punchline: He narrates his 'straightening' in real time via increasingly 'basic' markers: craving beer → basic references → finding Billy Eichner (a gay celebrity known for outspokenness) 'mean.'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best sustained jokes. The idea that straightness manifests as decreasing cultural sophistication — beer, basic references, finding Billy Eichner 'mean' — is both original and perfectly structured. The escalating specificity of 'I think Billy Eichner is mean' as proof of heterosexuality is inspired. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: 'Howard you like me now, Dad?' — George Michael after revealing the Fakeblock deal.
jokes.setup: George Michael has been maneuvering to sell Fakeblock to the Howard family (Rebel's family), partially as revenge on Michael for the Rebel situation.
jokes.punchline: 'Howard you like me now, Dad?' — a wordplay on 'How do you like me now?' using the Howard family name, delivered as a triumphant punchline.
jokes.explanation: Sharp wordplay that simultaneously functions as a punchline, a character reveal (George Michael's calculating side), and a Bluth-style zinger. The proper noun substitution is elegant and the emotional subtext (George Michael's resentment of Michael) gives it weight.

jokes.text: 'Know what you have in common with my new dad? You could both use a hit.' — George Michael to Michael, before punching him.
jokes.setup: George Michael is about to turn violent against his father.
jokes.punchline: 'You could both use a hit' — operating simultaneously as a music industry term (Ron Howard is a film producer; George Michael is invoking 'hit' in that context) and as a threat of physical violence. Then he actually hits Michael.
jokes.explanation: The wordplay is elegant: 'hit' as music industry term (comparing Ron Howard to Michael's father, both need a commercial success) and 'hit' as physical violence (which immediately follows). The narrator's 'And that's when, in my opinion, George Michael went too far' adds a wonderful meta layer. Among the episode's best. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: 'And that's when, in my opinion, George Michael went too far.' — Narrator, after the punch line/punch.
jokes.setup: George Michael has just said 'You could both use a hit' and punched Michael.
jokes.punchline: 'In my opinion' — the narrator breaking from omniscient neutrality to offer a personal judgment is both meta and funny, suggesting Ron Howard has been personally offended by the punch line (targeting him).
jokes.explanation: The narrator inserting 'in my opinion' is a meta-joke about Ron Howard's role as narrator — the 'new dad' comparison triggered a personal response. It's a perfect button to the George Michael punch sequence. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: We need something like this in China. 'Cause your game shows are the worst.
jokes.setup: Investors explain why they want Fakeblock
jokes.punchline: Buried among reasons about democracy suppression: 'your game shows are the worst' — as if American game show quality is a legitimate national security concern
jokes.explanation: Already scored as part of the 25:54 sequence — included separately here as a distinct standalone callback of the game show line. Scored slightly lower in isolation as the novelty has passed.

jokes.text: But I would rather have a brother who's alive and maybe bisexual than dead and straight!
jokes.setup: Buster is panicking about Gob being trapped in the cement; he pleads for the forms to be removed
jokes.punchline: 'I would rather have a brother who's alive and maybe bisexual than dead and straight' — a beautifully constructed false dilemma that reveals Buster has some uncertainty about the transformation's completeness
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quotable lines. The false dilemma structure — alive-bisexual vs. dead-straight — is perfectly Buster: he's prioritized the wrong variable (sexual orientation) in the survival equation, and 'maybe bisexual' suggests he's not fully confident the wall trick works. The whole Gob sexuality arc is condensed into one brilliant plea. High quotability, strong character integration.

jokes.text: 'But I would rather have a brother who's alive and maybe bisexual than dead and straight!' / 'I declare a mistrick!'
jokes.setup: Gob is encased in cement and not responding to the signal. Buster panics.
jokes.punchline: Buster's hierarchy of preferences ('alive and maybe bisexual' > 'dead and straight') is absurd but Buster-logical. 'Mistrick' — portmanteau of 'mistrial' and 'trick' — is a perfect Buster-invented legal-magic term.
jokes.explanation: Two exceptional jokes in one moment. 'Alive and maybe bisexual rather than dead and straight' is a perfect Buster value hierarchy — he's prioritizing his brother's life but the 'maybe bisexual' qualifier shows his confused understanding of Gob's situation. 'Mistrick' is an inspired coinage — Buster applying courtroom language to magic in a moment of panic. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: The narrator: 'That's how Gob, years before anyone else, ended up with an immovable position supporting a wall.'
jokes.setup: Gob is literally encased in cement inside a wall.
jokes.punchline: 'An immovable position supporting a wall' — physical (he's literally stuck in the wall) and political (anticipating/mocking the wall-building political stance that became dominant).
jokes.explanation: AD narrator wordplay at peak form. 'Immovable position supporting a wall' works simultaneously as physical description (cemented in) and political satire (the wall debate). 'Years before anyone else' adds the temporal irony of Gob accidentally pioneering a political stance. Among the episode's most elegant writing.

jokes.text: George to Chinese investors about Fakeblock: 'I don't want you to take on too much risk. And I wouldn't know what to charge you, sir.' / 'You will just sign it over, and your debt will be forgiven.'
jokes.setup: George is supposed to be negotiating from strength — Fakeblock is valuable.
jokes.punchline: George's 'negotiation' consists of undervaluing the product and expressing concern for the investors — and the investors immediately name their terms (sign it over for free, debt forgiven), having correctly identified a pushover.
jokes.explanation: George's incompetent negotiation is a good character beat — his concern for the investors' risk while sitting on a potentially valuable asset is perfectly backwards Bluth logic.

jokes.text: But onstage, it was Gob's face that was at risk. / Don't use the sledgehammer. / Get him out of there. / Did somebody say... Wonder?
jokes.setup: Buster panics about extracting Gob from the cement; someone calls for help
jokes.punchline: Tony Wonder emerges with his signature 'Did somebody say... Wonder?' — the callback to his catchphrase in a genuinely dramatic moment deflates the tension while confirming he's alive
jokes.explanation: Tony Wonder's catchphrase arrival at the exact right moment of crisis is perfectly timed. It simultaneously resolves multiple storylines and is a satisfying callback. The 'face at risk' setup (literal and reputational) is a nice narrator move.

jokes.text: 'My God, they're such a bunch of drama thugs.' — Tony Wonder on the Gay Mafia.
jokes.setup: Gob explains the Gay Mafia made him think Tony was dead.
jokes.punchline: 'Drama thugs' — combining 'drama queens' (gay slang) with 'thugs' (organized crime) into a single accurate descriptor for a group of gay gangsters who are also community theater actors.
jokes.explanation: 'Drama thugs' is a perfect portmanteau that captures everything about the Gay Mafia characters established earlier. Two words that do enormous descriptive work. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: 'Well, I might not have been dead, but my career was. I was doing three shows a day in Branson in the, uh, Yakov Smirnoff Theater.' / 'What's he like?' / 'Disappointing. Disa... Uh, disappointing. Yeah, he treats you like an audience member.'
jokes.setup: Tony explains the nadir of his career after the Gay Mafia canceled his contract.
jokes.punchline: The specific humiliation of the Yakov Smirnoff Theater in Branson is funny; Gob's sincere curiosity about Yakov Smirnoff ('What's he like?') is funnier; 'Disappointing... he treats you like an audience member' — implying Yakov only treats audience members like strangers, not performers — is the perfect button.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's funniest exchanges. Yakov Smirnoff Theater in Branson is already a comedically specific location. Gob's sincere interest in Yakov's personality is a perfect character beat. But 'he treats you like an audience member' — delivered as a complaint by a performer — is the clincher. The implication that Yakov Smirnoff treats everyone with equal audience-member disinterest is inspired. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I guess I can go back to Joni Beard now.' Tony: 'And I might make it official with Sally Sitwell.' — both announcing they'll pursue women now that they're 'straight.'
jokes.setup: Gob and Tony are celebrating their return to heterosexuality.
jokes.punchline: Both their 'straight' options are women who are adjacent to each other's worlds (Joni Beard = John Beard's wife/relative; Sally Sitwell = political ally), and neither seems particularly enthusiastic.
jokes.explanation: Setup for what follows — both men's lack of conviction in their heterosexual declarations is the joke, but it's mostly setup for the 'just hands' sequence.

jokes.text: 'I guess, uh, friendship is out of the question, then.' [long pause] 'But, I mean, we could do other stuff.' 'Yeah. Like with our hands.' 'Just hands.' 'I'd like that.' 'Me, too.' 'Just hands!'
jokes.setup: Gob and Tony have both announced women they'll pursue — now facing that as business competitors, they're saying goodbye.
jokes.punchline: Their farewell immediately devolves from 'friendship is out of the question' → 'other stuff' → 'with our hands' → 'just hands' → mutual agreement → emphatic 'Just hands!' The escalating specificity of 'just hands' while supposedly committing to heterosexuality is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The 'just hands' sequence is a comedic escalation masterclass. Each step — 'other stuff,' 'hands,' 'just hands,' mutual agreement, emphatic repetition — builds the absurdity while both characters maintain they're straight. The joke is entirely in what goes unspoken. The structure (each beat slightly more specific and more damning) is near-perfect. Among the episode's best moments.

jokes.text: The narrator reveals the Fakeblock deal: 'Great idea to have me lose that tooth when I hit the ground. It worked great.' / 'And we printed that in less than two hours.' — Michael and George Michael celebrating the con.
jokes.setup: Michael and George Michael appear to have had a violent falling out.
jokes.punchline: It was staged — the fight, the tooth, the whole thing. 'We printed that in less than two hours' refers to a fake tooth. The Bluth family con is revealed.
jokes.explanation: The reveal that the father-son fight was staged is satisfying misdirection — it recontextualizes the earlier fight scene and confirms Michael and George Michael actually are working together. 'We printed that in less than two hours' is a great detail.

jokes.text: 'God knows where he dug that up.' — George Michael, about Buster finding the mannequin.
jokes.setup: Gob explains he asked Buster to throw down a mannequin for the illusion.
jokes.punchline: 'God knows where he dug that up' — 'dug up' is an idiom, but given that Buster presumably obtained the mannequin from wherever Tony Wonder's body was stored, 'dug up' is potentially literally true.
jokes.explanation: Elegant double meaning — 'dug that up' as idiom vs. potentially literal. The rewatch bonus is significant because on first viewing you don't realize what this might be implying.

jokes.text: 'Well, you know, I'm thinking about doing, like, a solo show. Maybe do some close-up magic. A little sleight of...' / 'Foot.' / 'Oh. Is it that obvious?'
jokes.setup: Gob is describing his magic ambitions — 'a little sleight of hand—'
jokes.punchline: George Michael completes it as 'foot' — Gob has a hook for a hand (Buster's hook), making sleight of hand impossible. The visual gag of Gob with a hook/missing hand has been a recurring AD element.
jokes.explanation: Wait — this is actually about Buster wearing the hook, not Gob. George Michael's 'foot' implies Gob is missing a hand (he borrowed Buster's hook?) or that Buster's hook situation affects Gob's magic plans. The economy of 'Foot' as the complete punchline is exceptional — a single word that does all the work. Rewatch essential.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, I Ali-Baba-ed a hand, but at least they sent me a lefty.'
jokes.setup: The conversation about 'sleight of foot' reveals hand-related complications.
jokes.punchline: Buster has 'Ali-Baba-ed a hand' — an invented term suggesting he acquired a hand through some unspecified crime (Ali Baba = theft/magic). 'At least they sent me a lefty' implies he ordered or received a replacement hand, and it wasn't his preferred side.
jokes.explanation: Outstanding. 'Ali-Baba-ed a hand' is an invented verb that implies both magic trick and theft, and the nonchalance of 'at least they sent me a lefty' — treating acquiring a human hand like a mail-order complaint — is deeply wrong and deeply funny. Buster-specific to the point of being untranslatable to any other character. This is what 9-range craft looks like. Justifies 9.

jokes.text: Narrator on Fakeblock's uselessness: 'You wanna see Ben Affleck's peen? Because after installing his homemade software into George Michael's computer... It's gone. Yep, and forever.'
jokes.setup: Murphybrown has installed his own software on George Michael's computer, believing he fixed Fakeblock.
jokes.punchline: Rather than fixing Fakeblock, he permanently destroyed the only copy of a leaked Ben Affleck sex tape — and the narrator offers this as a practical demonstration of the software's capabilities.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Ben Affleck's peen' as the Fakeblock test case is inspired. The narrator's casual framing — as if this is the most practical demonstration possible — is perfect deadpan. Somewhat topical (2018 era celebrity) but the joke structure is strong.

jokes.text: 'So, basically, you came up with a way to destroy content?' / 'Yep.' / 'Damn it.'
jokes.setup: George Michael realizes what Murphybrown has actually accomplished with his 'fix.'
jokes.punchline: Fakeblock — the privacy software the family has been trying to sell for multiple seasons — turns out to be a content destruction tool. Michael's 'Damn it.' absorbs the full weight of this revelation.
jokes.explanation: The seasons-long Fakeblock arc culminates in the revelation that it destroys content. 'Damn it.' as the complete response is perfect. The anti-climax is the joke.

jokes.text: 'All right. Well, we're back to poor.' / 'Looks like you two will be sharing a sleeping bag tonight.'
jokes.setup: The family's financial situation has collapsed again.
jokes.punchline: 'Sharing a sleeping bag' — a callback to Tobias's sleeping bag situation earlier (DeBrie and Murphybrown left him) and also a simple image of poverty.
jokes.explanation: Functional callback that closes the financial desperation loop. 'Back to poor' is a satisfying three-word summary.

jokes.text: 'Oh, nobody's gonna build a wall. According to the succeeding New York Times, Donald Trump doesn't even have a... a chance of winning that election.' / 'Oh, I don't know, don't count out his likability.'
jokes.setup: The characters are consoling themselves that the wall project failing isn't so bad.
jokes.punchline: The dramatic irony — the audience knows Trump won — makes Michael's certainty ('doesn't even have a chance') land as comedy. George Michael's 'don't count out his likability' adds a second ironic layer.
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony does the heavy lifting — the joke is mostly delivered by history. 'Don't count out his likability' is funnier than the setup. Character integration is weak (this could be anyone). Rewatch bonus because the dramatic irony intensifies.

jokes.text: 'Oh, I suppose someone should let toe-quotes "Rebel" know that the deal is off, right?' / 'Yeah... gotta... gotta say thanks to her.'
jokes.setup: The deal to sell Fakeblock fell through, and someone needs to tell Rebel Alley.
jokes.punchline: 'Toe-quotes' — Michael does finger quotes with his toes (inferred), and 'say thanks to her' — the revelation that Rebel was never in on the con and was actually helpful.
jokes.explanation: 'Toe-quotes' is a delightful word — implying Michael is doing air quotes with his toes, which is both physically absurd and perfectly Michael. The setup for the Rebel/Maeby reveal.

jokes.text: 'But, of course, Rebel wasn't in on the con. Maeby was.'
jokes.setup: The audience (and Michael) assumes Rebel was part of the Fakeblock con.
jokes.punchline: It was Maeby all along — the teenager-turned-schemer. The reveal recontextualizes everything.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's reveal is economical and satisfying. Maeby as the secret mastermind is deeply in-character and rewards her earlier appearances in the episode.

jokes.text: 'Then they'll want something for being heroes.' / 'Well, too late. I already did hear you.' / 'They're here for their paychecks.'
jokes.setup: The family is discussing whether the protesters/heroes will want compensation.
jokes.punchline: George Michael has already been overheard, and the 'heroes' are literally there for cash payment — deflating any heroic narrative.
jokes.explanation: Functional beat — the heroes-for-hire subversion is amusing but not among the episode's strongest jokes.

jokes.text: Maeby's ask for protest payment: 'I'll settle for a bedroom with half bath.'
jokes.setup: The protesters are expecting payment.
jokes.punchline: Maeby settles for a bedroom with half bath — real estate instead of money, and specifically modest real estate.
jokes.explanation: Maeby negotiating in real estate terms with 'half bath' as a specific detail is a characteristically AD-specific joke. The domesticity of the demand is funny.

jokes.text: Buster: 'I was hoping it would bring out Lucille Austero. But the trial is done! I mean, why do we care? You don't really think I had anything to do with her missing, do you? Do you?'
jokes.setup: Buster reveals his reason for organizing protesters was to find Lucille 2.
jokes.punchline: His escalating defensive questions — 'why do we care? You don't really think I had anything to do with her missing, do you? Do you?' — make him sound extraordinarily guilty while apparently trying to sound innocent.
jokes.explanation: Buster's defensive spiral is perfect cringe comedy — each question meant to reassure is more incriminating than the last. The double 'Do you?' is the ideal anxious button.

jokes.text: 'We were just speculating as to where Lucille 2 ended up. Uh, we assumed she'd be here to protest. Well, maybe there's a surprise under one of these burqas.'
