jokes.setup: They're discussing where Lucille 2 might be.
jokes.punchline: Buster suggests she might be disguised in a burqa among the protesters — delivered with Buster's characteristic guileless confidence.
jokes.explanation: Sets up the Lucille 2 reveal. The 'surprise under a burqa' suggestion is dark in retrospect — it actually IS something important. Rewatch bonus for the foreshadowing.

jokes.text: Lindsay emerges from under a burqa: 'Hi.' / 'Wait, so, at the... Protester... That was you kicking me...' / 'In the balls.' / 'Yeah, your mother was kicking me in the balls.'
jokes.setup: Someone has been kicking Tobias during the protest. He's been assuming it was Lucille.
jokes.punchline: It was Lindsay — disguised in a burqa, kicking her ex-husband in the balls during a political protest. Tobias's correction 'your mother was kicking me in the balls' (to Buster, about Lindsay, not Lucille) is delivered with weary matter-of-factness.
jokes.explanation: The 'your mother was kicking me in the balls' line is both literally accurate (Lindsay is Buster's adopted sister/quasi-mother figure in the show's tangled relationships) and physically specific. The matter-of-fact delivery of ball-kicking as a protest activity is perfectly Tobias.

jokes.text: 'How often do you get to do that when you're holding one of these?' — Buster, displaying his hook hand while being told his mother pushed Mimi down the stairs.
jokes.setup: In the middle of a serious family revelation, Buster has his hook hand visible.
jokes.punchline: Buster's reaction to learning the truth is to make a gesture with his hook and remark on the rarity of that opportunity — completely failing to engage with the emotional content.
jokes.explanation: Buster's emotional avoidance manifesting as hook-hand appreciation is deeply in character. In a moment where he should react to a family revelation, he's distracted by a gestural opportunity. The hook gag is deployed perfectly — not as a throwaway but as character revelation.

jokes.text: How often do you get to do that when you're holding one of these?
jokes.setup: Lindsay reappears; Buster is holding something (apparently a protest sign or similar object)
jokes.punchline: Buster's innocent comment about the fun of doing something while 'holding one of these' — which, given that he has a hook for a hand, carries an implicit question about all his activities
jokes.explanation: A Buster joke that plays on his physical limitations. The specific object isn't entirely clear from transcript, but the implication about hook-hand challenges is the joke. Functional but not one of the episode's stronger moments.

jokes.text: 'Your anger spread to me, made me a horrible partner to Tobias.' / 'And I will say you weren't the most loving mommy yourself.' / 'But to be fair, you barely knew Murphybrown, so...'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is confronting her biological mother about their shared history.
jokes.punchline: 'But to be fair, you barely knew Murphybrown' — Lindsay qualifying her accusation with the mitigating factor that her mother also barely knew her own child is both absurd and revealing.
jokes.explanation: The generosity of 'to be fair' applied to bad mothering is Lindsay-specific comedy — she's already finding excuses for the mother who abandoned her.

jokes.text: 'So, you were a critical, difficult mother, but I gotta be honest, Lucy, you're an amazing sister! Half-sister.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has been cataloguing her biological mother's failures.
jokes.punchline: The pivot from 'terrible mother' to 'amazing sister' to the immediate correction 'Half-sister' lands three beats: the illogical comfort, the surprising warmth, and the immediate qualification.
jokes.explanation: The quick correction of 'Half-sister' after the warm 'amazing sister' landing is a classic AD button — the family's inability to not qualify their moments of warmth. Strong three-beat structure.

jokes.text: 'Maeby really wanted to roll her eyes, but not as much as she wanted the hug.' — Narrator, on Maeby's reaction to the family reunion.
jokes.setup: The family is having a warm reunion moment — which Maeby typically disdains.
jokes.punchline: The narrator articulates Maeby's internal conflict: teenage cynicism vs. genuine desire for family warmth. 'Not as much as she wanted the hug' is the concession.
jokes.explanation: The narrator breaking down Maeby's competing desires is genuinely touching and funny. 'Not as much as she wanted the hug' is a perfectly calibrated beat — it's sweet without being sentimental, because the narrator frames it as a contest Maeby lost.

jokes.text: 'Huh? Nothing breaks us apart.' / 'What happened to Michael and George Michael?' / 'It's like they never showed up.' / 'Where the hell are they going?' / 'Probably pulling the car around.'
jokes.setup: The family is celebrating their togetherness after the reunion.
jokes.punchline: Michael and George Michael are conspicuously absent. The family cycles through excuses before settling on 'pulling the car around' — a polite fiction that they've left.
jokes.explanation: 'Pulling the car around' as the family's agreed-upon fiction for Michael abandoning them is perfectly in character. The family's instantaneous pivot from 'nothing breaks us apart' to 'where did Michael go' is great irony.

jokes.text: 'Actually, I think they might be ready to move on.' / 'Right. Michael always comes back.' / 'Yeah. He thinks he has to "bring us together."' / 'Oh, Michael is such a zero.' / 'Oh, he'll be back.' / 'Michael always comes back.' / 'Oh, he'll be back. Especially after something goes horribly wrong, based on something stupid we did.'
jokes.setup: The family discusses whether Michael has really left.
jokes.punchline: The family's complete confidence that Michael will return ('Michael always comes back') combined with Gob's meta-accurate prediction ('after something goes horribly wrong, based on something stupid we did') is both a character joke and a structural comment on the entire series.
jokes.explanation: This is the show commenting on its own premise — Michael 'coming back' when the family does something stupid is the entire series. The family's self-awareness that they'll generate another crisis and Michael will return is both funny and oddly poignant. 'Michael is such a zero' followed by absolute confidence he'll return frames the show's core dynamic perfectly. Justifies 8.5.

jokes.text: Right. Michael always comes back. Yeah. He thinks he has to 'bring us together.' Oh, Michael is such a zero. Oh, he'll be back. Michael always comes back.
jokes.setup: The family discusses Michael's departure
jokes.punchline: The family cycling through affectionate contempt — 'such a zero,' 'he'll be back' — while being entirely dependent on him is the show's central irony in miniature
jokes.explanation: A great closing family beat — 'Michael is such a zero' followed by 'he'll be back' perfectly captures the Bluth family's relationship with Michael. They need him, don't respect him, and absolutely know he'll return. Especially resonant given the show's own on-again, off-again relationship with renewal.

jokes.text: 'No, no. I don't think anything's gonna go horribly wrong for a long, long ti—' [cuts off]
jokes.setup: The narrator attempts to reassure the audience that things will be fine.
jokes.punchline: The narrator cuts off mid-word — the universe itself interrupting the reassurance — before cutting to the 'On the next Arrested Development' stinger revealing Gob is entombed in a wall.
jokes.explanation: The mid-sentence cut is a well-executed structural joke — the narrator's confident reassurance is interrupted by the imminent revelation of disaster. Economy and structure are the strengths.

jokes.text: Post-credits 'On the next AD': The family discovers Gob's mannequin in the wall is actually a body. 'Hey, gang, does Buster's mannequin look like...' / 'Judy Garland.' / 'Is it Lucille 2?' / 'Why is she smiling?' / 'This isn't a mannequin.' / '...' / 'This is... the happiest corpse I've ever seen.'
jokes.setup: The wall is being dismantled. A mannequin Buster threw in is being retrieved. Someone wonders if it looks like someone familiar.
jokes.punchline: 'The happiest corpse I've ever seen' — Lucille 2's body is in the wall, and she's smiling. The horror + 'happiest corpse' + the completeness of the reveal = devastating final joke.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best-constructed joke and the show's most effective dark comedy moment in this episode. Every element converges: the wall (built around Gob), the mannequin (which Buster obtained), Lucille 2 (missing for seasons), and the sublime 'happiest corpse I've ever seen' — which implies Lucille 2 died joyfully or was posed smiling. The escalation ('Judy Garland' → 'Is it Lucille 2?' → 'This isn't a mannequin' → 'the happiest corpse') is a textbook dark comedy reveal. The callback density (wall plot, Buster body-dumping, Lucille 2 arc) makes this a rewatch essential. Justifies 9+.

jokes.text: 'Okay, I did it. So...' — Buster's non-confession/confession, after seeing the body.
jokes.setup: Everyone has just discovered what appears to be Lucille 2's body in the wall.
jokes.punchline: Buster's response is 'Okay, I did it. So...' — an admission with the trailing 'So...' suggesting he's waiting to see what happens next, as if confessing to murder is just another conversational move.
jokes.explanation: The series' darkest and most economical joke. 'Okay, I did it. So...' is a complete murder confession with the energy of someone who just admitted to eating the last cookie. The 'So...' is everything — it implies Buster has been here before, isn't sure what the next step is, and treats the admission as routine. Four words at the end of the episode that recontextualize everything. Perfect callback to the opening 'I'm just a boy.' Maximum economy. Justifies 9+

jokes.text: Okay, I did it. So...
jokes.setup: After the revelation of Lucille 2's smiling corpse in the wall
jokes.punchline: Buster's anticlimactic 'Okay, I did it. So...' — a casual confession, trailing off, as if admitting to a minor infraction rather than murder
jokes.explanation: A perfect final joke for Buster and possibly for the episode. 'Okay, I did it. So...' — the 'So...' trailing off into nothing — is Buster's complete confession to murder delivered with the energy of admitting he ate the last of the cereal. The 'So...' is doing extraordinary work: it suggests he expects negotiation, or a response, or that this changes very little. Maximum character integration: only Buster could confess to murder this way. Economy is perfect. An all-time AD moment.

jokes.text: 'But doesn't she remind you of Mom?' — Final line of the episode.
jokes.setup: The family is staring at the smiling corpse of Lucille 2 in the wall.
jokes.punchline: Buster finds Lucille 2's corpse reminiscent of Lucille — either because of physical resemblance or because he's drawn to powerful older women even in death.
jokes.explanation: The episode's final line and the single best joke. It captures the entire Buster/Lucille/Lucille 2 dynamic in six words — his Oedipal confusion, his attraction to Lucille 2 as a mother substitute, and the dark comedy of finding a smiling corpse reminiscent of his mother. The delivery is presumably as casual as every other Buster line, which makes it perfect. This line functions as a series thesis statement disguised as a murder-scene non-sequitur. Perfect character integration, perfect economy, perfect dark comedy. Justifies the highest craft score in the episode.

tmdb_overview: As the Bluths come together to unveil a wall, they once again discover that they deserve nothing more than to be saddled with each other forever.
insight: Final act escalation delivers 62 jokes in 58 minutes—series-high density for a denouement.
